title: Psalms 141
subtitle: Set a Guard over My Mouth

sections.header: Verses 1–4 — “Let My Prayer Be Set Before You Like Incense”

heb.paragraph: "Let my prayer be set before you like incense (qəṭōreṭ); may the lifting up of my hands be like the evening sacrifice" (v.2). Prayer as incense: rising, fragrant, offered at the appointed time. Revelation 5:8 identifies the incense-bowls in heaven as the prayers of the saints. David asks God to guard his mouth and keep watch over the door of his lips (v.3). The request is for divine speech-control: not my willpower but your guard.

notes.note: "Let my prayer be set before you like incense." Prayer as sacred fragrance rising to God. The evening sacrifice: prayer at the same hour the temple lamb was offered. Revelation 5:8 connects incense to prayer.

"Set a guard over my mouth, LORD; keep watch over the door of my lips. Do not let my heart be drawn to what is evil." Guard over the mouth, watch over the lips, restraint on the heart. Three layers of protection against sin’s entry points.

refs.note: The angel offers incense with the prayers of the saints—prayer as fragrant offering.
I want people everywhere to pray, lifting up holy hands.
Open my lips, LORD, and my mouth will declare your praise.

hist.context: Psalm 141 is an evening prayer psalm. David asks that his prayer rise like incense and his lifted hands like the evening sacrifice. The psalm then turns to moral vigilance: 'Set a guard over my mouth, LORD; keep watch over the door of my lips.' This concern for speech integrity pervades the Davidic psalms of this collection.

sections.header: Verses 5–10 — “Let a Righteous Man Strike Me”

heb.paragraph: "Let a righteous man strike me — that is a kindness (ḥesed); let him rebuke me — that is oil on my head" (v.5). David prefers the wound of a friend to the kiss of an enemy (Prov 27:6). Correction from the righteous is an anointing, not an insult. The psalm closes with trust: "But my eyes are fixed on you, Sovereign LORD; in you I take refuge — do not give me over to death" (v.8).

notes.note: "Let a righteous man strike me — that is a kindness." Correction reframed: a blow from a friend is love; flattery from an enemy is poison. David welcomes the painful truth.
"My eyes are fixed on you, Sovereign LORD; in you I take refuge." The gaze of Psalm 123 returns: eyes fixed upward, refuge claimed, death refused.

refs.note: Wounds from a friend can be trusted; faithful correction is kindness.
If someone is caught in sin, restore them gently—righteous rebuke.
Discipline seems painful but produces a harvest of righteousness.

hist.context: David welcomes rebuke from the righteous: 'Let a righteous man strike me—that is a kindness.' He refuses the hospitality of the wicked. The psalm's close focuses on the snares and traps set by evildoers, with confidence that they will fall into their own nets. The imagery of eyes fixed on the LORD Sovereign shows where true safety lies—not in human alliances but in divine protection.

ppl.name: David
ppl.text: David is a key figure in Set a Guard over My Mouth.

rows.label: The Righteous Wound — 141:5–10
rows.text: "Let a righteous man STRIKE me — that is kindness." Eyes fixed on God. Refuge taken.

hebtext.note: "Let my prayer be set before you like incense (qəṭōreṭ); may the lifting up of my hands be like the evening sacrifice" (v.2). Prayer as incense: rising, fragrant, offered at the appointed time. Revelation 5:8 identifies the incense-bowls in heaven as the prayers of the saints. David asks God to guard his mouth and keep watch over the door of his lips (v.3). The request is for divine speech-control: not my willpower but your guard.

"Let a righteous man strike me — that is a kindness (ḥesed); let him rebuke me — that is oil on my head" (v.5). David prefers the wound of a friend to the kiss of an enemy (Prov 27:6). Correction from the righteous is an anointing, not an insult. The psalm closes with trust: "But my eyes are fixed on you, Sovereign LORD; in you I take refuge — do not give me over to death" (v.8).

themes.note: Worship peaks: prayer as incense and evening sacrifice. Faith: welcoming correction as kindness. Rev 5:8 connects incense-prayers to heaven.

prayer_prompt: Set a guard over my mouth; keep watch over the door of my lips. Consider asking God about your speech — what guard you need, what words need stopping.
